#9 Walking from Setagaya to Suginami, Tokyo

Areas discussed: Japan, Tokyo, Setagaya City, Suginami City, Tsukayama Park

December 6, 2023 1 hour 20 minutes

All transcripts are machine generated.

Transcript

But I got all set, got my little sweet-cut card out as already, and it's that same problem I was talking about is just not hungry enough. I can't force it. A chocolate aid for breakfast just does not warn off.

I got to walk further, I got to go further before I can get anything else. And I love them just on one of these like overpasses that goes over one of the big major streets. These things are always really weathered and really worn down, but they're just cool, I really like them.

Oh man, this is a big one, this is a six-lane highway, eight lanes if you count the parking parts, which I don't know if those counts are probably not. Anyway, I was walking past the 7-11, it's like, okay, not time yet, not yet.

And what I noticed, because I'm always just sort of interested in how your language center develops for languages, you know, where. I just remember like going to French school as a kid. It's not like my pronunciation is perfect, but there were words that I could say that my mom couldn't. Stuff like the word for leaf is fey. And she just couldn't say it right, but I didn't know how to articulate to her as a kid, like what she's saying wrong. But when I think about it with my tongue fey, yeah, yeah, you know, you're not just saying fey. You got to throw in little acrobatics with your tongue. But then on the other hand, if you get too deep into it, you know, like a native French person can run circles around what I can do. I even noticed it. I did an episode of my writing podcast. It's called How to Write a Novel. I did an episode while I was in Montreal. And a guy stopped me real briefly. All he did was ask me like, hey, is this such and such a thing up here? And even just the way he said that and all I said is, hey, I don't know, I'm not from Montreal. And he just said, like, yeah, I duck all. But even when I listen back, I'm like, man, yeah, he's saying that with a French accent that I can't pull off. You know, I'm in the middle somewhere. But there's benefits to being in the middle. Because I do some stuff I hadn't realized I was doing until my Toronto friends kindly mocked me for it. But I don't pronounce the H in front of words. Human, H-U-N-A-N, I just say human. And I never thought that was weird. And they were like, why don't you say human, human. And even just I was like, what do you mean? What's the difference? But it turns out there is a human. But I have to force it so hard. To me, human, human being, it's so easy to say human. I feel like I'm Hank Hill, hobbobbing. Like I got to force it. I'm like, I'm not fucking doing that. But once I got pointed out to me, sometimes I make a little bit of an effort now that I'm aware of it. But that's something, you know, with obviously the French floating through my brain. They would never pronounce an H to save their fucking lives. So now they're do I?

But on the other hand, if you got too much of it, too big a dose of the French. That is when I low-key that is when you talk like this, you're a French person. That is only so much you can do about it. You can't talk, you know, within a North American English accent anymore. It's gone. So as is a common trope, I don't know much about Japan really. But I do know they have fewer sounds than us. A-U-A-O, and that every word has to end with that sound. And like my friend Brad, when he was here doing language courses, you know, he's saying stuff like, like if you, even if you're in McDonald's and the context clues are very clear, if you ask for a Big Mac combo, forget it, it's not a combo. It's called a set, but you can't say set. It has to end with I-U-A-O. So Big Mac Set-O. Okay, I could probably do that. But then he said like, yeah, but it's not Set-O. It's Set-O. I think I could be wrong. And you'd think like, okay, I'm in McDonald's, I'm basically saying the thing. But again, I guess that's fair. Because when you think of English words, I mean, all it takes is one little sound and it's a whole different word. It really can be easily confusing. But anyway, what I'm trying to ramble about is that there are just fewer sounds in the Japanese language. They've got sounds we don't have. And then for us, you know, I suppose like, I don't know what's an example. Like those African click languages, like they do stuff that we don't do, or rolling your ears in Spanish languages, or clearing your throat in Middle Eastern, or oftentimes French as well. I wonder who's got the most sounds, like who is the most able to do everybody else's language. But in any Japan, that's her damn sure. And I just thought it was funny that this kid who is losing her shit, she is like not in control of herself anymore, having a total tantrum breakdown. And yet, I didn't notice this at first, but because I was putting stuff in my book bag, standing there for a minute, I heard enough of it that you start to hear the sounds of Japanese words in the screams. Like even while she's just expressing noise to make noise, to just be like, I'm pissed, it still doesn't drift into. She's not going to accidentally make the same sounds a North American English-speaking toddler would make.

It's already, like the lines are already drawn in the sand. These are the sounds I have access to, even when I'm freaking out.

And I just think that's interesting, one interesting thing that I never thought about.

And it's probably for the best that it never crossed my mind. Like we should test this. We should make people real upset, or we should set up a horror movie scenario. I'm not a person who does these things, but I do find them interesting. Oh man, here's another. This one is a really old house. Where this one will continue to be about whatever this one is about. Random ill-informed person wanders around foreign city, rambles incoherent thoughts. That's what this podcast should really be called.

But here's a little slap a little creation theory on your creator theory. It sort of seems backward, but I think when you're setting out to do something, to create something like a podcast, say, you know, the conventional wisdom would be, have a goal, try to get somewhere. But I don't think so. I think it's better not to have a goal you're trying to achieve, because it just adds a lot of stress, a lot of pressure, and you're not going to reach it. Whatever your goal is, I want X number of whatever. The world is unfathomably large. There is just a number of people making stuff all the time that is impossible to even comprehend. The number of hours of material uploaded to YouTube every minute would blow your fucking mind. I won't even try to make up a statistic because it's already at a date. That anything gets through at all, that anyone ever finds anything that you're doing is a fucking miracle. So I think it's better to approach these things is to do them for yourself, like a hobby, and then if it goes somewhere great, but that's not the point. So in this case, I'm really just doing this because I wish I had done it last time. Whatever random audio I happened to accidentally have from my last visit to Japan, I would go back and listen to it. And I was like, I like that. I like listening to it because for me, it's extremely evocative. You know, I'm here. I'm in the place. So when I listen back to this, I'm going to remember. I mean, not specifically. I mean, like, where the hell am I right now? The fuck is this? But in general, I will remember. So even if not a single other human being ever listened to this. For me, this will be cool. Editing episodes, even after I'm not in Japan anymore. If I want a vicariously vacation, I'll have these. And I can listen back and be like, what is he talking about? What is this?

But I think that's the best way to approach stuff. Figure out your own personal reason. What you can get out of this. And it's just like, it's like a hobby. It's like collecting stamps or making a scrapbook or quilting or knitting, whatever. I just make podcasts. And then eventually people do kind of find them and people do listen. And maybe it's useful to somebody. And if someone else is like, I hate this. This is the worst thing I ever heard. That's fine. And that's fine. You know, that's another thing, I guess, is like, take the feedback and take advice and try to change things so people will like it better. 50:00 No, I never do that. I never do that. And maybe it'll just make my stuff be obscure forever. But that's not what I'm here for. It's not what I'm trying to do.

I'm not trying to make a bunch of people watch this or listen to it. These are on YouTube, too, by the way.

I always just toss my podcasts on YouTube because they generally get less attention as a YouTube video than they do as a podcast. But everyone's in a while. YouTube just picks something up and suddenly it gets a bunch of views and then 99.9% of people don't like it. But you know, a few little people find it and they stick around. And it's slowly a cruise. But yeah, if you do something just for yourself, it's going to be extremely idiosyncratic. It's not going to be for most people. But at the same time, you can't fail. You did it. You know, if you did the thing you want, then you did it.

12-hour difference. But I found this big ravine with walking paths on either side. And then as you walk down it, every once in a while there's a walking bridge that goes across. And not just because of the kilometers thing. I do think probably I've internalized miles a little better in my mind than kilometers.

So then the other question is, since I don't really want to walk all the way back, yeah, once I see it on a map too, like I'm still. I'm still in the thick of it. I'm not even close to, I mean, of course, you can't not be in Tokyo. You know, unless I go way, way west, like there were times when I went so stupid far west that it really was like where the heck am I. But it's weird the way Tokyo is laid out that it just goes so far west that you're just in the countryside basically. It's like you're not even there anymore. In Tokyo, proper, of course. You're just surrounded by Tokyo. There is no escape. So there's fears that there wouldn't be any trains around, like as if. But let's see if I can find one. What do we get? Although I got all afternoon. Now that I've sat down a bit and I'm a bit rested, it is nice to walk at night too. Night is very pretty in a different way. I don't know what's better day or night time. I mean, I guess night time is prettier, but daytime is nicer because, you know, there's sun. But maybe, maybe I will walk back in the night, take a different route. But let's see, if I was going to take a train, I assume those are on here. What do we got?

In fact, if I just keep walking up this ravine, I'm extremely close to Tokaido Station. Don't know what that is or where it goes, but hey, whatever, it's a start. And yeah, the Japanese subway system is extremely complicated. But once you get on it, you can usually at least get to somewhere major. And again, since my Airbnb is pretty close to Shibuya, I mean, trying to get somewhere obscure is a little tough, but if you're just trying to get to a major station, they'll be signed somewhere, pointing me in the general direction. Should I do that? But I usually don't. Generally, I just walk. Although, for now, like I said, I'm just gonna hunker down here for a bit, but I'm gonna keep walking. I'm gonna keep walking up the Kanda River even more.

Oh yeah, so I almost forgot the whole point while I was bringing all this crap up. So yeah, I got here to this park and all these washrooms. And I was like, you know, not just in time, but getting close to just in time. And I head in, and there's the urinals, and then there's the one little stall, and inside is a squat toilet. And that's where I was just glad that I've been to Japan before, and I've gone through the whole squat toilet ritual already. I'm glad this isn't the first time that I ever was presented with a squat toilet. 1:15:00 Because I don't mind them at all. People complain about squat toilets. I don't care. I'm just glad there's a toilet. A toilet to toilet, man. And this is fine. I don't quite get squat toilets. I assumed there was some reason why they're so basic that maybe it made it easier to function or a simpler mechanism, but not really. You still got to flush it. It basically seems like a toilet just without a place where you can sit. Just a toilet in the wrong shape, you know. But what I learned before, this is pretty much all you need to know about a squat toilet, but I didn't realize this at first. Just make sure you pull your pants down, past your knees, way down. So then when you squat your pockets don't turn upside down, because I definitely did that the first time. Luckily nothing fell out into the toilet, but if you got coins or your wallet or your phone, you're in danger of it falling out of your pockets. And you're in danger of pooping on your clothing. So just be careful. I mean, you're always in danger of that, I guess. Yeah, just pull all the way down as far as you can, and then you just squat. And the thing that it surprised me is, you know, I walk a lot. So I've always had good walking muscles. I was surprised last time I visited Japan how little that translated to squatting. Squatting was so hard on my legs and like my upper size. And I'm just like, fuck, this hurts. There's definitely like squat toilets are fine as long as everything goes smoothly. If you ever were constipated, I don't know, man. Holy. Geez, squat toilet. That's when it would be unpleasant, because there's like a limited amount of time that a person can squat.

So now, not only do I still walk a lot, but because it pains me if I sit too long because of this cyst that I have through my miraculous good fortune, I do a lot of standing now. If I have an opportunity for like a standing desk situation instead of sitting, I'll always stand. I stand a lot and my standing muscles have become incredibly better. I can stand for a very long time before I get tired. So I was like, this will definitely translate to squatting. And it's just so interesting how non transferable some of your muscles are. It helped some. My legs didn't hurt as bad as they used to, but they still hurt. I still was like, man, squatting still sucks. You have to develop squatting muscles independently of your other leg muscles. It just doesn't work.

But anyway, the squat toilet saves the day. Don't worry about the terrible fear of squat toilets. It's fine. It's not nearly as bad as anybody says. I'll take a squat toilet over no toilet any day, although it is funny speaking of OG station.

At OG station, there is like an ancient old washroom there with a squat toilet that I did use once. And then I realized over nearer to where you get on the train, there's a super duper mega brand new, beautiful modern washroom with the western style toilets as well as all the bidet options and all the cool tech. And I remember it was like, man, like, I've had Airbnb's that don't have this where they just have a normal boring toilet, a normal boring apartment toilet. You can go to OG station and have a better experience. That happened many times, in fact. High gym station, I remember two, just probably a lot of stations, just have these amazing washrooms. And that is some Japan shit, dude. If you ever have to use the bathroom in the New York subway, you're taking your life into your hands. If you ever need to use the bathroom in Toronto or Vancouver or Montreal, too fucking bad because it doesn't exist. Toronto might have washrooms at Young Street, maybe. Japan has tons of washrooms and oftentimes the subway bathroom, the fucking subway bathroom is better than your toilet at home. It's fucking awesome. But squat toilets, no biggie. That's my lesson for today.
